schools.summary: Abraham old and blessed: 'and Abraham was old, and well stricken in age: and the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things' — Abraham now advanced in years (about a hundred and forty, Isaac being forty, 25:20, the aged patriarch, near the close of his life, the setting for his care to provide a wife for Isaac, vv.2-4, before his death, the old age of the father of the faithful); 'the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things' (God having blessed the patriarch in everything, in substance, in his son, in his long life, in the covenant, the fulfillment of the promise of blessing, 12:2, the temporal and spiritual blessing on Abraham, the faithful God crowning his servant's life with blessing); the LORD's blessing Abraham 'in all things' setting the stage for the chapter (the aged, blessed patriarch now seeking, by God's guidance, a wife for the son of promise, that the blessing might continue in the seed, the providence of God over the household, the blessing carried forward through Isaac and Rebekah) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham was old, and well stricken in age: and the LORD had blessed Abraham in all things
Abraham's old age and the LORD's blessing him in all things
the aged patriarch near the close of his life (25:20)
God's blessing on Abraham (12:2), carried forward through the seed

schools.summary: The oath gesture: 'and Abraham said unto his eldest servant of his house, that ruled over all that he had, Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh' — Abraham charging his chief, trusted servant (the steward over all his house, generally taken to be Eliezer of Damascus, 15:2, the faithful servant entrusted with the great commission of finding a wife for Isaac, the steward of authority and trust); 'Put... thy hand under my thigh' a solemn form of oath (also used by Jacob, 47:29) variously understood — (a) a token of subjection, the servant swearing under his master's authority; (b) a reference to the covenant of circumcision (the 'thigh'/loins a euphemism, the oath sworn by the sign of the holy covenant between Abraham and God); (c) a reference to the promised Seed — Christian writers conceiving that the gesture pointed to the Messiah, the promised Seed who should come from Abraham's loins (swearing by him, or by the hope of the seed, cf. Hebrews 7:9-10), the solemn, sacred form of oath, binding the servant by what was most sacred to Abraham (the covenant and the promise); the gesture marking the solemnity of the charge (Abraham binding his servant by a sacred oath, whatever the precise meaning, the oath resting on the covenant and the promise, the gravity of the commission to find a wife for the son of promise) — Ellicott, Keil, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What did the oath-gesture 'hand under my thigh' signify?

sides.position: A token of subjection - the servant swearing under his master's authority
A reference to the covenant of circumcision - the oath sworn by the sign of the holy covenant between Abraham and God
A reference to the promised Seed - Christ, who should come from Abraham's loins (the Christian conception)

themes: And Abraham said unto his eldest servant... Put, I pray thee, thy hand under my thigh
the oath gesture (the crux of 'thy hand under my thigh')
a token of subjection vs the covenant of circumcision vs the promised Seed (Christ)
the solemn, sacred oath binding the servant by what was most sacred

schools.summary: The prohibition of a Canaanite wife: 'and I will make thee swear by the LORD, the God of heaven, and the God of the earth, that thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell' — Abraham binding the servant by oath, swearing by the LORD (the Maker and possessor of heaven and earth, 14:22, the only proper object of an oath, the solemn swearing by the true God, the gravity of the charge); Isaac not to marry 'of the daughters of the Canaanites' — because the Canaanites were under the curse (the curse of Canaan, 9:25, devoted to destruction/slavery, it would have been inconsistent with prudence and with God's design to unite the child and heir of the promise with one under a curse, the separation of the seed of promise from the accursed, idolatrous line, the avoidance of the unequal yoke, the danger of idolatry and corruption, as later forbidden, Deuteronomy 7:3, the marrying 'in the faith,' within the covenant family); 'among whom I dwell' (though Abraham dwelt among the Canaanites, he would not have his son marry among them, the patriarch keeping the seed separate from the corrupt nations, the care to preserve the covenant-line from idolatry and the curse, the godly concern for a wife of the true faith and kindred) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

debates.question: Why was Isaac forbidden a Canaanite wife?

sides.position: The Canaanites under the curse (9:25), idolatrous, devoted to destruction - the heir of promise not to be unequally yoked with the accursed line (Deuteronomy 7:3), the seed kept separate, the wife to be of the covenant kindred and faith

themes: And I will make thee swear by the LORD... that thou shalt not take a wife unto my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, among whom I dwell
the prohibition of a Canaanite wife (the crux of the separation)
the Canaanites under the curse (9:25), idolatrous - the unequal yoke avoided (Deuteronomy 7:3)
the seed kept separate, the wife to be of the covenant kindred and faith

schools.summary: The charge to seek a wife from the kindred: 'but thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac' — Abraham charging the servant to go to his own country (Mesopotamia, Haran) and to his kindred (the family of Nahor, 22:20-23) to find a wife for Isaac (the seeking of a wife from the covenant family, not the Canaanites, v.3, the care for a wife of the true kindred and faith); the wife to be sought from Abraham's 'country and kindred' (the family of Nahor in Mesopotamia, from whom Rebekah, the granddaughter of Nahor, would come, 22:23, the seeking within the family that, though imperfect, Laban's idolatry, 31:19, retained some knowledge of the true God, the wife of the kindred, kept from the Canaanite line); 'take a wife unto my son Isaac' (the commission to find a wife for the son of promise, the providence of God to be sought in the matter, vv.7, 12-14, the marriage of Isaac, through which the covenant-line would continue, the care of the godly father for the marriage of his son in the faith) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: But thou shalt go unto my country, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son Isaac
the charge to seek a wife from Abraham's kindred
the family of Nahor in Mesopotamia (22:20-23), from whom Rebekah would come
the marriage of the son of promise in the faith

schools.summary: The servant's prudent question: 'and the servant said unto him, Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land: must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?' — the servant prudently raising the possibility that the woman might refuse to leave her home and follow him to Canaan (the faithful servant's careful foresight, considering the difficulties of his commission, the prudent inquiry before undertaking the oath); the servant asking, 'must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?' (if the woman would not come, must Isaac go to her, back to Mesopotamia? the careful working out of the terms of the commission, the servant seeking clear instructions, the question that drew out Abraham's firm answer, vv.6-8); the servant's question showing his fidelity and prudence (he would understand his charge fully before swearing, the careful, conscientious servant, the foresight that anticipated difficulties, the question answered by Abraham's insistence that Isaac must not return, vv.6-8) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And the servant said unto him, Peradventure the woman will not be willing to follow me unto this land: must I needs bring thy son again unto the land from whence thou camest?
the servant's prudent question about the woman's willingness
the faithful servant's careful foresight, seeking clear instructions
the question drawing out Abraham's firm answer (vv.6-8)

schools.summary: Abraham forbids taking Isaac back: 'and Abraham said unto him, Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again' — Abraham firmly forbidding the servant to take Isaac back to Mesopotamia (whatever the woman's choice, the emphatic charge, 'beware,' the patriarch's insistence that the son of promise remain in the land of promise, the care to keep Isaac in Canaan); the emphatic warning, 'beware' (Abraham strongly charging the servant on no account to take Isaac out of the land, the firmness of the patriarch, the importance of keeping the heir in the land of promise); 'bring not my son thither again' (Isaac not to leave Canaan, the land of promise, given to the seed, v.7, to take him back to Mesopotamia would be to forsake the land of promise and endanger the covenant-walk, the care to keep the son of promise in the promised land, in separation from the kindred's idolatry, the faith that held Isaac to the land God had given) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB, Calvin.

themes: And Abraham said unto him, Beware thou that thou bring not my son thither again
Abraham's firm charge not to take Isaac back
keeping the son of promise in the land of promise (v.7)
the faith that held Isaac to the land God had given

schools.summary: Abraham's faith in the guiding angel: 'the LORD God of heaven, which took me from my father's house, and from the land of my kindred, and which spake unto me, and that sware unto me, saying, Unto thy seed will I give this land; he shall send his angel before thee, and thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence' — Abraham recalling God's call (12:1), his word, and his oath (the promise of the land to his seed, 12:7; 15:18; 13:15, the ground of his confidence, the God who had led and promised would guide the matter); 'he shall send his angel before thee' (Abraham's faith that God would send his angel to guide the servant and prosper his way, the providence of God ordering the marriage, the angel, whether a created angel, or the Angel of the LORD, sent to prepare the way, the confidence that God would direct the servant to the right wife); 'thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence' (Abraham assured that the servant would succeed, God would guide him to a wife from the kindred, the faith resting on God's promise and providence, the certainty of the godly that God would order the matter for the good of the covenant-seed) — Gill, Clarke, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: The LORD God of heaven... he shall send his angel before thee, and thou shalt take a wife unto my son from thence
Abraham's faith that God would send his angel before the servant
the recollection of God's call and oath (12:1, 7)
the confidence in God's guiding providence over the marriage

schools.summary: The servant released if the woman refuse: 'and if the woman will not be willing to follow thee, then thou shalt be clear from this my oath: only bring not my son thither again' — Abraham providing that, if the woman refused to come, the servant would be released from his oath (the just, reasonable condition, the servant not bound to the impossible, the careful framing of the oath); the servant 'clear' (discharged, free) from the oath if the woman would not follow (the reasonable limit of the obligation, the conscientious framing of the charge, that the servant be not bound beyond what was possible); 'only bring not my son thither again' (whatever happened, the one absolute condition that Isaac must not be taken back to Mesopotamia, v.6, the firm insistence on keeping the son in the land of promise, the single unalterable term, the care to keep Isaac in Canaan) — Gill, Calvin.

themes: And if the woman will not be willing to follow thee, then thou shalt be clear from this my oath: only bring not my son thither again
the release of the servant from the oath if the woman refuse
the reasonable, careful framing of the oath
the one firm condition: Isaac must remain in the land of promise (v.6)

schools.summary: The servant takes the oath: 'and the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master, and sware to him concerning that matter' — the servant performing the oath-gesture (v.2) and swearing to do as Abraham had charged (concerning the wife for Isaac, the solemn binding of the servant, the faithful steward accepting the great commission by oath, the matter sealed); the servant doing as Abraham bade (v.2), putting his hand under the thigh — the solemn oath-form (whatever its precise meaning, v.2, the binding of the servant by the sacred oath, the conscientious acceptance of the charge); 'sware to him concerning that matter' (the servant swearing to carry out the commission, to seek a wife for Isaac from the kindred, not the Canaanites, vv.3-4, and not to take Isaac back, vv.6, 8, the faithful servant bound to his task, the great undertaking sealed by oath, the steward setting out, v.10) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the servant put his hand under the thigh of Abraham his master, and sware to him concerning that matter
the servant taking the oath
the faithful steward accepting the great commission by oath
the matter sealed - to seek a wife from the kindred and keep Isaac in the land

schools.summary: The servant's journey to the city of Nahor: 'and the servant took ten camels of the camels of his master, and departed; for all the goods of his master were in his hand: and he arose, and went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor' — the servant taking ten camels (for the journey, the gifts, and to bring back the bride and her company) and setting out, with his master's goods (gifts and provisions, vv.22, 53) at his disposal (the well-furnished journey, the steward entrusted with his master's wealth for the mission); 'all the goods of his master were in his hand' (the servant having charge of a portion of his master's goods, to provide gifts for the bride and her family, vv.22, 53, the trust reposed in the faithful steward, the means for the honorable suit); 'went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor' (the servant journeying to Mesopotamia, Aram-naharaim, the land between the rivers, to the city of Nahor, Haran, or a city where Nahor's family dwelt, the journey to the kindred, vv.3-4, the providence guiding him to the family of Nahor, Rebekah's people, 22:20-23) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And the servant took ten camels of the camels of his master, and departed... and went to Mesopotamia, unto the city of Nahor
the servant's journey to the city of Nahor
the faithful steward entrusted with his master's goods for the mission
the journey to the kindred of Nahor (22:20-23)

schools.summary: The servant halts at the well: 'and he made his camels to kneel down without the city by a well of water at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water' — the servant resting his camels (making them kneel, as camels do) outside the city, by the well (the well, the place where the women came to draw water, a natural place to meet the daughters of the city, the prudent choice of place); the servant coming 'at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water' (the evening, when the women came to the well to draw water, the custom of the East, the prudent timing, that he might meet the maidens of the city, the place and time chosen to find a wife); the servant's halting at the well, at the time of drawing water, setting the scene for the providential meeting (vv.12-15, the place where, by God's guiding, he would find Rebekah, the prudent arrangement joined with prayer, v.12, and trust in providence) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: And he made his camels to kneel down without the city by a well of water at the time of the evening, even the time that women go out to draw water
the servant halting at the well at the time of drawing water
the prudent choice of place and time to meet the maidens
the scene set for the providential meeting and his prayer (vv.12-15)

schools.summary: The servant's prayer for good speed: 'and he said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, I pray thee, send me good speed this day, and shew kindness unto my master Abraham' — the servant praying to the LORD, the God of his master Abraham (the true God, whom Abraham served, the faithful servant a man of prayer, seeking God's help in his task, the addressing of God as Abraham's God, the covenant God, the piety of the steward); the servant praying 'send me good speed this day' (that God would prosper his way, give him good success in his errand, the seeking of God's guidance and blessing on the day's work, the prayer of one who would follow providence and seek God's direction, the dependence of the servant on God); 'shew kindness unto my master Abraham' (the servant pleading God's kindness to Abraham, for his master's sake, in fulfillment of the promise, the prayer grounded on God's covenant love to Abraham, the seeking of God's mercy and faithfulness, the servant's care for his master's interest, and his faith in God's covenant kindness) — Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And he said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, I pray thee, send me good speed this day, and shew kindness unto my master Abraham
the servant's prayer for good speed
the faithful servant a man of prayer, seeking God's guidance
the plea grounded on God's covenant kindness to Abraham

schools.summary: The servant stands by the well: 'behold, I stand here by the well of water; and the daughters of the men of the city come out to draw water' — the servant, in his prayer (v.12), describing his situation: standing by the well as the maidens of the city came out to draw water (the setting of his petition, the place and occasion for the sign he would ask, v.14); the servant standing by the well (the place where he would meet the maidens, the prudent placing, v.11, joined with prayer, the setting for the providential meeting); 'the daughters of the men of the city come out to draw water' (the maidens of the city coming to the well in the evening, v.11, among them the one God had appointed for Isaac, v.14, the occasion for the sign, the providence about to bring Rebekah, v.15) — Gill.

themes: Behold, I stand here by the well of water; and the daughters of the men of the city come out to draw water
the servant standing by the well as the maidens come to draw
the place and occasion for the sign he would ask (v.14)
the providence about to bring Rebekah (v.15)

schools.summary: The servant's proposed sign: 'and let it come to pass, that the damsel to whom I shall say, Let down thy pitcher, I pray thee, that I may drink; and she shall say, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also: let the same be she that thou hast appointed for thy servant Isaac; and thereby shall I know that thou hast shewed kindness unto my master' — the servant proposing a sign: the maiden who, asked for a drink, would offer to water his camels also, let her be the one God had appointed for Isaac (the sign chosen not at random, but one that would reveal the character of the bride, her kindness, courtesy, humility, and industry — to water ten thirsty camels a labor of generous hospitality, the prudent, fitting test); the servant's asking a sign lawful faith and prudent prayer, not a presumptuous tempting of God (it is the comfort and belief of a good man that God's providence extends to the smallest occurrences, it is lawful, while we set God's will before us as our rule, to pray that God would, by hints of providence, direct us in the way of our duty, the servant not rashly tempting God, but, in dependence and prayer, seeking God's guidance by a sign that itself tested the bride's fitness, the prayer of faith following providence; a more cautious view noting that such signs are not to be lightly multiplied, lest we tempt God, and that this was an extraordinary case, guided by the Spirit, but the servant's sign, joined with prayer and reason, generally approved as faith, not presumption); 'thereby shall I know that thou hast shewed kindness unto my master' (the servant looking to the sign as God's answer, the token of his guidance and kindness, the faith that God would direct him to the right wife, the seeking of God's will, not the servant's own) — Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: Was the servant's asking a sign faith, or a tempting of God?

sides.position: Lawful faith and prudent prayer - following providence, seeking God's direction by a sign that itself tested the bride's character (the dominant reading)
Signs must not be lightly multiplied lest we tempt God - an extraordinary case, Spirit-guided (the more cautious view)

themes: And let it come to pass, that the damsel... shall say, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also: let the same be she that thou hast appointed for thy servant Isaac
the servant's proposed sign (the crux of asking a sign)
lawful faith and prudent prayer, following providence, testing the bride's character vs the caution not to tempt God
the sign that revealed the bride's kindness and industry

schools.summary: The speedy answer - Rebekah comes: 'and it came to pass, before he had done speaking, that, behold, Rebekah came out, who was born to Bethuel, son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham's brother, with her pitcher upon her shoulder' — even before the servant had finished his prayer (vv.12-14), Rebekah coming out (the speedy, immediate answer to prayer, the providence of God going before the request, Isaiah 65:24, 'before they call, I will answer,' the remarkable readiness of God's answer); Rebekah 'born to Bethuel, son of Milcah, the wife of Nahor, Abraham's brother' (she of the very kindred Abraham had named, the granddaughter of Nahor, 22:20-23, the providence bringing the one of the right family, the answer not only swift but exact — the maiden of Abraham's kindred); 'with her pitcher upon her shoulder' (Rebekah coming to draw water, her pitcher on her shoulder, the setting for the sign, v.14, the maiden, industrious and humble, coming to the well, the providence bringing her at the very moment) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, before he had done speaking, that, behold, Rebekah came out... with her pitcher upon her shoulder
the speedy answer - Rebekah coming before the prayer was done
the swift, exact answer to prayer (Isaiah 65:24), the maiden of the kindred (22:20-23)
God bringing the right one at the very moment

schools.summary: Rebekah's beauty and chastity: 'and the damsel was very fair to look upon, a virgin, neither had any man known her: and she went down to the well, and filled her pitcher, and came up' — Rebekah described as very beautiful, a virgin, pure and chaste (the threefold note of her fairness, virginity, and purity, the suitable bride for Isaac, the providence bringing a maiden fair and chaste, the honorable character of Rebekah); the emphasis on Rebekah's purity — 'a virgin, neither had any man known her' (the chaste, undefiled maiden, the fit bride for the son of promise, the honor of her virtue); 'she went down to the well, and filled her pitcher, and came up' (Rebekah coming to the well and drawing water, the industrious, humble maiden, a woman of substance, yet drawing water herself, the setting for the sign, v.14, the providence bringing her to the very place) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And the damsel was very fair to look upon, a virgin, neither had any man known her: and she went down to the well, and filled her pitcher, and came up
Rebekah's beauty and chastity
the maiden fair, chaste, and industrious, the fit bride for the son of promise
the providence bringing her to the very place (the setting for the sign, v.14)

schools.summary: The servant asks Rebekah for water: 'and the servant ran to meet her, and said, Let me, I pray thee, drink a little water of thy pitcher' — the servant hastening to meet Rebekah and asking her for a little water (the first part of the sign he had proposed, v.14, the eager testing of the sign, the courteous request, the servant putting the providential token to the proof); the servant 'ran' (his eagerness to test the sign and find the appointed wife, the readiness to follow the providence, the prompt action); 'Let me... drink a little water of thy pitcher' (the very request of the proposed sign, v.14, the asking of a drink, to see if she would offer to water the camels also, the putting of the sign to the proof, the courteous, humble request) — Gill.

themes: And the servant ran to meet her, and said, Let me, I pray thee, drink a little water of thy pitcher
the servant running to ask Rebekah for water (the beginning of the sign)
the eager putting of the proposed sign (v.14) to the proof
the courteous request to see if she would water the camels also

schools.summary: Rebekah gives the servant drink: 'and she said, Drink, my lord: and she hasted, and let down her pitcher upon her hand, and gave him drink' — Rebekah answering courteously ('Drink, my lord') and quickly letting down her pitcher to give him water (the maiden's kindness, courtesy, and readiness, the first part of the sign, v.14, fulfilled, the gracious, humble response); Rebekah's respectful, courteous answer ('my lord') — the modest, kind manner of the maiden (the courtesy that marked her character, the gracious reply to the stranger); 'she hasted, and let down her pitcher upon her hand, and gave him drink' (Rebekah promptly and kindly giving the servant drink, the readiness and humility of the maiden, letting down her pitcher to her hand for him, the sign beginning to be fulfilled, v.14, the kindness that would extend to the camels, v.19) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And she said, Drink, my lord: and she hasted, and let down her pitcher upon her hand, and gave him drink
Rebekah's courteous giving the servant drink
the maiden's kindness, courtesy, and readiness
the first part of the sign (v.14) fulfilled

schools.summary: Rebekah offers to water the camels: 'and when she had done giving him drink, she said, I will draw water for thy camels also, until they have done drinking' — Rebekah, of her own accord, offering to draw water for the camels too, until they had finished (the very sign the servant had proposed, v.14, now fulfilled to the letter, the maiden's generous, industrious kindness, the providential token given); the greatness of the offer — to water ten thirsty camels (each drinking much) a great labor of generous hospitality (the maiden's willing, industrious kindness, far beyond what was asked, the character revealed by the sign, her kindness, humility, and diligence); 'until they have done drinking' (Rebekah drawing until all the camels were satisfied, the thoroughness of her kindness, the full, generous service, the exact fulfillment of the sign, v.14, the providence confirming her as the appointed bride) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And when she had done giving him drink, she said, I will draw water for thy camels also, until they have done drinking
Rebekah offering to water the camels (the sign fulfilled)
the maiden's generous, industrious kindness, far beyond what was asked
the exact fulfillment of the very sign the servant proposed (v.14)

schools.summary: Rebekah waters all the camels: 'and she hasted, and emptied her pitcher into the trough, and ran again unto the well to draw water, and drew for all his camels' — Rebekah quickly emptying her pitcher into the trough and running back to the well, drawing water again and again until all the camels were watered (the great labor and diligence, to water ten thirsty camels required many trips, the industrious, willing service, the kindness fulfilled in deed); Rebekah's haste and repeated running (the energetic, willing diligence, the maiden sparing no labor, the kindness shown in earnest, laborious service); 'drew for all his camels' (Rebekah watering every one of the ten camels, the full, thorough kindness, the sign, v.14, completely fulfilled, the character of the bride displayed in her generous, diligent service, the providence confirming her) — Gill.

themes: And she hasted, and emptied her pitcher into the trough, and ran again unto the well to draw water, and drew for all his camels
Rebekah's diligence in watering all the camels
the energetic, willing, laborious service sparing no labor
the sign (v.14) completely fulfilled, the character of the bride displayed

schools.summary: The servant silently marks the providence: 'and the man wondering at her held his peace, to wit whether the LORD had made his journey prosperous or not' — the servant watching Rebekah in silent wonder (at the exact fulfillment of his sign, vv.14, 19-20, he holding his peace, observing, neither speaking nor hindering, the silent, attentive marking of the providence, the wonder at God's answer); the servant 'wondering' (marveling at the maiden's kindness and the exact correspondence with his sign, the silent astonishment at the providence, the attentive watching to see if this were indeed God's answer); 'to wit whether the LORD had made his journey prosperous or not' (the servant watching to know, to ascertain, whether God had indeed prospered his way and answered his prayer, the careful, prayerful discernment of the providence, the servant not presuming, but waiting to be sure, confirming her family, vv.23-24, the attentive marking of God's hand) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Clarke.

themes: And the man wondering at her held his peace, to wit whether the LORD had made his journey prosperous or not
the servant silently marking whether God had prospered his way
the silent wonder at the exact fulfillment of his sign
the careful, prayerful discernment of the providence, waiting to be sure

schools.summary: The servant gives Rebekah golden gifts: 'and it came to pass, as the camels had done drinking, that the man took a golden earring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands of ten shekels weight of gold' — the servant giving Rebekah valuable golden ornaments (an earring or nose-ring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets of ten shekels weight of gold, the gifts of betrothal/honor, tokens of his master's wealth and the honorable suit, the rich presents marking the importance of the occasion); the golden ornament (an earring, or, as many hold, a nose-ring) and the heavy gold bracelets (the costly gifts, the customary tokens of honor and betrothal in the East, the wealth of Abraham displayed); the heavy gold bracelets (ten shekels) rich gifts — tokens of the honorable suit and of Abraham's substance (the servant, now persuaded, vv.21, 26-27, that Rebekah was the appointed bride, honoring her with the betrothal-gifts, the providence confirmed, the suit begun) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, Calvin.

themes: And it came to pass, as the camels had done drinking, that the man took a golden earring of half a shekel weight, and two bracelets for her hands of ten shekels weight of gold
the servant giving Rebekah the golden gifts
the rich betrothal-gifts, tokens of Abraham's wealth and the honorable suit
the servant now honoring the appointed bride (vv.21, 26-27)

schools.summary: The servant asks Rebekah's family and for lodging: 'and said, Whose daughter art thou? tell me, I pray thee: is there room in thy father's house for us to lodge in?' — the servant asking Rebekah whose daughter she was (to learn her family, whether of Abraham's kindred) and whether there was room to lodge (the inquiry to confirm the providence, vv.21, 24, the seeking of lodging and of the family connection); 'Whose daughter art thou?' (the servant asking her family, to learn whether she was indeed of Abraham's kindred, vv.3-4, the careful confirming of the providence, the inquiry that would reveal her to be of the family of Nahor, v.24); 'is there room in thy father's house for us to lodge in?' (the servant seeking lodging for himself and his company, the practical request, the seeking of hospitality from the family, the way opened to the house of Bethuel, vv.28-32, the providence guiding toward the kindred) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And said, Whose daughter art thou? tell me, I pray thee: is there room in thy father's house for us to lodge in?
the servant asking Rebekah's family and for lodging
the careful confirming of the providence (whether of the kindred, vv.3-4)
the way opened toward the house of Bethuel

schools.summary: Rebekah declares her kindred: 'and she said unto him, I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, which she bare unto Nahor' — Rebekah telling the servant her family: she the daughter of Bethuel, the son of Milcah and Nahor (Abraham's brother, 22:20-23, the confirmation that she was indeed of Abraham's kindred, vv.3-4, the providence fully confirmed — the maiden of the very family Abraham had named); Rebekah the granddaughter of Nahor (22:23) — of the very family Abraham had sent the servant to (vv.3-4, the exact answer to the servant's prayer and sign, the providence bringing the right one); 'the son of Milcah, which she bare unto Nahor' (Rebekah's lineage traced to Nahor, Abraham's brother, 22:20, the full confirmation of the providence, the servant now sure that God had prospered his way, v.21, the appointed bride of the kindred found) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And she said unto him, I am the daughter of Bethuel the son of Milcah, which she bare unto Nahor
Rebekah declaring herself of Abraham's kindred
the maiden of the very family Abraham had named (22:20-23)
the exact answer to the servant's prayer, the providence confirmed

schools.summary: Rebekah offers lodging and provision: 'she said moreover unto him, We have both straw and provender enough, and room to lodge in' — Rebekah, beyond answering his question (v.23), offering hospitality: there was straw and fodder enough (for the camels) and room to lodge (for the men, the maiden's continued kindness and courtesy, the generous offer of the family's hospitality); Rebekah assuring the servant of ample provision for the camels (straw and provender, the abundance of the household, the kindness extending to his beasts as well as his company); 'and room to lodge in' (Rebekah offering lodging for the servant and his men, the hospitable welcome, the kindness of the maiden, and the family, the way opened to the house, vv.28-32, the providence continuing to prosper the servant's errand) — Gill.

themes: She said moreover unto him, We have both straw and provender enough, and room to lodge in
Rebekah offering lodging and provision
the maiden's continued kindness and hospitality
the way opened to the house (vv.28-32)

schools.summary: The servant bows in worship: 'and the man bowed down his head, and worshipped the LORD' — the servant, assured now that God had prospered his way (vv.21, 24-25), bowing his head and worshipping the LORD (the immediate, reverent thanksgiving, the pious servant turning at once to worship and praise, the acknowledgment of God's hand in the providence); the servant bowing his head — the gesture of reverent worship and gratitude (the humble adoration, the piety of the steward, ready to worship upon the answer); 'worshipped the LORD' (the servant worshipping God in thanksgiving for the answered prayer and the guidance, the prompt, devout praise, the example of the godly, returning thanks at once for God's mercy, the worship that breaks into the blessing of v.27) — Gill, Clarke, Calvin.

themes: And the man bowed down his head, and worshipped the LORD
the servant bowing in worship to the LORD
the pious servant's immediate, reverent thanksgiving for the answered prayer
the example of the godly, returning thanks at once for God's mercy

schools.summary: The servant blesses the LORD for his leading: 'and he said, Blessed be the LORD God of my master Abraham, who hath not left destitute my master of his mercy and his truth: I being in the way, the LORD led me to the house of my master's brethren' — the servant blessing God for not failing his master, but showing him mercy and truth, faithfulness (God's mercy, his kindness, and his truth, his faithfulness to the promise, joined in the providence, the praise for God's covenant-faithfulness to Abraham); the great word — 'I being in the way, the LORD led me to the house of my master's brethren' (the servant acknowledging God's providential leading — being in the way, set out on his errand, doing his duty, in dependence and prayer, the LORD leading, guiding, directing him to the very house of Abraham's kindred, the doctrine of providence — that God guides those who, doing their duty and seeking him, walk in the way, 'in all thy ways acknowledge him, and he shall direct thy paths,' Proverbs 3:6, God's providence extending to the smallest occurrences, leading his servant exactly to the right place, the comfort of the godly that God leads those who follow him in the way of duty); 'the LORD led me to the house of my master's brethren' (God guiding the servant precisely to the family of Nahor, Abraham's kindred, vv.3-4, 24, the exactness of the providence, the answer to prayer, the leading of God confirmed) — Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

debates.question: What does 'I being in the way, the LORD led me' teach of providence?

sides.position: God guides those who, doing their duty and seeking him, walk in the way (Proverbs 3:6) - his providence extending to the smallest things, leading the servant exactly to the right house; the comfort of the godly in following providence

themes: Blessed be the LORD God of my master Abraham, who hath not left destitute my master of his mercy and his truth: I being in the way, the LORD led me to the house of my master's brethren
the servant blessing the LORD for his providential leading (the crux of providence)
God's mercy and truth (faithfulness) to Abraham, joined in the providence
'I being in the way, the LORD led me' - God guides those who walk in the way of duty (Proverbs 3:6)

schools.summary: Rebekah runs to tell her household: 'and the damsel ran, and told them of her mother's house these things' — Rebekah running home and telling her mother's household what had happened (the meeting, the gifts, the servant's words, the maiden's eager report, the news brought to the family, and to Laban, v.29, the household of the women, 'her mother's house,' the women's quarters); Rebekah 'ran' (her eager haste to tell the wonderful news, the excitement at the meeting and the gifts, the prompt reporting to her family); 'told them of her mother's house these things' (Rebekah telling the women of her mother's house, the family, the news that brought Laban out to the servant, v.29, the report that opened the way to the family's reception, vv.29-32, the providence proceeding toward the betrothal) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And the damsel ran, and told them of her mother's house these things
Rebekah running to tell her household
the maiden's eager report of the meeting and the gifts
the news that would bring out Laban (v.29) and open the way to the family's reception

schools.summary: Laban runs out to the servant: 'and Rebekah had a brother, and his name was Laban: and Laban ran out unto the man, unto the well' — Rebekah's brother Laban running out to meet the servant at the well (Laban, who would later figure largely in the history of Jacob, chs. 29-31, here introduced, the brother coming to receive the stranger, the family's response to Rebekah's news, v.28); 'Laban ran out' (his eager haste to meet the servant, whether moved by hospitality, or, as some hint, seeing the gifts, v.30, by an eye to the wealth, the prompt welcome of the stranger, the brother going out to receive him); 'unto the well' (Laban going out to the servant at the well, where he still stood with the camels, v.30, the meeting that led to the invitation and reception, vv.31-32, the family's welcome of the servant, the providence proceeding) — Ellicott, Gill, Henry, Wesley.

themes: And Rebekah had a brother, and his name was Laban: and Laban ran out unto the man, unto the well
Laban running out to the servant
the brother's eager haste (moved by hospitality, or, as some hint, by the gifts, v.30)
the welcome that would receive the servant into the house (vv.31-32)

schools.summary: What moved Laban - the gifts and the words: 'and it came to pass, when he saw the earring and bracelets upon his sister's hands, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister, saying, Thus spake the man unto me; that he came unto the man; and, behold, he stood by the camels at the well' — Laban coming when he saw the golden gifts (v.22) on Rebekah and heard her account (the gifts, a sign of the stranger's wealth, noted as part of what moved Laban, some, with the later history of his covetousness, chs. 29-31, seeing here a hint of his eye to gain, the brother's response to the gifts and the news); Laban also hearing Rebekah's words ('Thus spake the man unto me') — her account of the meeting (the report that, with the gifts, brought him out, the family informed and moved to receive the servant); 'behold, he stood by the camels at the well' (Laban finding the servant still standing by the camels at the well, waiting, v.21, the meeting, the way to the invitation, v.31, the providence proceeding toward the reception) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, JFB.

themes: And it came to pass, when he saw the earring and bracelets upon his sister's hands, and when he heard the words of Rebekah his sister... that he came unto the man
what moved Laban to come - the gifts and Rebekah's words
the gifts (v.22) moving Laban (some seeing a hint of his later covetousness, chs. 29-31)
the servant found still waiting by the camels at the well (v.21)

schools.summary: Laban welcomes the servant: 'and he said, Come in, thou blessed of the LORD; wherefore standest thou without? for I have prepared the house, and room for the camels' — Laban welcoming the servant as 'blessed of the LORD' (recognizing, from the gifts and the account, that the man and his master were blessed of God, and perhaps that the family worshipped the same LORD, the courteous, hospitable invitation, the welcome into the house); Laban calling the servant 'blessed of the LORD' (acknowledging God's blessing on him, and on Abraham, the recognition of the true God, the LORD, Jehovah, in the family of Nahor, though later mixed with idolatry, 31:19, the courteous, pious greeting); 'I have prepared the house, and room for the camels' (Laban having made ready the house for the servant and room for the camels, the generous hospitality, the welcome and provision, the reception of the servant into the family's home, v.32, the providence proceeding toward the betrothal, vv.33-49) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley, Calvin.

themes: And he said, Come in, thou blessed of the LORD; wherefore standest thou without? for I have prepared the house, and room for the camels
Laban's welcome of the servant
the recognition of God's blessing on the servant and Abraham (31:19)
the generous hospitality, receiving the servant into the home (v.32)

schools.summary: The servant received, the camels provided for: 'and the man came into the house: and he ungirded his camels, and gave straw and provender for the camels, and water to wash his feet, and the men's feet that were with him' — the servant received into the house (the camels unloaded and fed, straw and provender, water given to wash the feet of the servant and his men, the hospitable reception, with provision for man and beast, the customary Eastern hospitality, washing the feet, 18:4); the camels unloaded and fed (the care for the beasts, the full hospitality of the household, the provision Rebekah had promised, v.25); 'water to wash his feet, and the men's feet that were with him' (the washing of the feet, the refreshment of travelers, 18:4; 19:2, the courteous, customary hospitality, the servant and his men received and refreshed, the way to the errand, v.33) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And the man came into the house: and he ungirded his camels, and gave straw and provender for the camels, and water to wash his feet, and the men's feet that were with him
the servant received into the house and the camels provided for
the hospitable reception, with provision for man and beast
the customary Eastern hospitality (washing the feet, 18:4)

schools.summary: The servant tells his errand before eating: 'and there was set meat before him to eat: but he said, I will not eat, until I have told mine errand. And he said, Speak on' — food set before the servant, but he would not eat until he had declared his errand (the faithful servant putting his master's business before his own refreshment, the diligence and fidelity of the steward, the errand first); the servant saying 'I will not eat, until I have told mine errand' (the example of faithful diligence — the servant's master's business coming before his own comfort, the conscientious steward, mindful of his charge, the zeal to accomplish the commission, and, having prayed and been answered, eager to bring it to its end, the faithfulness of the servant, a model of diligence in God's work); 'And he said, Speak on' (Laban, or Bethuel, bidding the servant speak, the leave granted to tell the errand, vv.34-49, the way opened for the servant's full account of his commission and the providence, the family ready to hear) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And there was set meat before him to eat: but he said, I will not eat, until I have told mine errand. And he said, Speak on
the servant's faithfulness in telling his errand before eating
the faithful steward putting his master's business before his own refreshment
a model of diligence in God's work, the family bidding him speak

schools.summary: The servant begins his account: 'and he said, I am Abraham's servant' — the servant beginning his account (vv.34-49) by declaring himself Abraham's servant (the faithful steward identifying himself and his master, the modest, plain opening, the beginning of the full narration of his commission and the providence); the servant naming himself 'Abraham's servant' (not boasting of himself, but honoring his master, the humble identification, the opening of the account that would set forth Abraham's blessing, v.35, the charge, vv.37-41, and the providence, vv.42-48); 'I am Abraham's servant' (the start of the servant's faithful, full recounting of all that had brought him there, the model of fidelity, telling his errand plainly, the account that would persuade the family, vv.50-51, the providence to be set forth) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And he said, I am Abraham's servant
the servant beginning his account, 'I am Abraham's servant'
the faithful steward identifying himself and honoring his master
the beginning of the full narration of his commission and the providence (vv.34-49)

schools.summary: The servant recounts the LORD's blessing of Abraham: 'and the LORD hath blessed my master greatly; and he is become great: and he hath given him flocks, and herds, and silver, and gold, and menservants, and maidservants, and camels, and asses' — the servant beginning his account (vv.34-49) by setting forth God's blessing of Abraham (the patriarch made great and wealthy by the LORD, 24:1, the blessing recounted to commend the match, that Isaac, the heir, was the son of a great and blessed man, the acknowledgment of God's hand in Abraham's prosperity); the enumeration of Abraham's wealth — flocks, herds, silver, gold, servants, camels, asses (the great substance God had given him, the temporal blessing on the patriarch, the wealth that marked Isaac as a worthy match for Rebekah); the servant ascribing all to the LORD ('the LORD hath blessed') — not to chance or Abraham's wit, but to God's blessing (the godly servant honoring God in recounting his master's prosperity, the blessing of God the ground of Abraham's greatness) — Gill.

themes: And the LORD hath blessed my master greatly; and he is become great: and he hath given him flocks, and herds, and silver, and gold...
the servant recounting the LORD's blessing of Abraham
Abraham made great and wealthy by the LORD (24:1)
all ascribed to God's blessing, commending the heir of so blessed a man

schools.summary: The servant tells of Isaac the heir: 'and Sarah my master's wife bare a son to my master when she was old: and unto him hath he given all that he hath' — the servant telling of Isaac's birth to Sarah in her old age (the miraculous son of promise, 21:2, the heir born against nature, by God's power, the son through whom the covenant-line would continue, the marking of Isaac as the child of promise); 'unto him hath he given all that he hath' (Abraham having made Isaac his sole heir, giving him all, 25:5, the son of promise the inheritor of all the patriarch's substance, and the covenant, the commending of Isaac as the heir, the worthy match for Rebekah); Isaac, the miraculous son of promise (21:2) and the heir of all (25:5), set forth as the bridegroom (the servant commending the match by the dignity of the heir, the covenant-line to continue through Isaac and the wife now sought) — Gill, Clarke, JFB.

themes: And Sarah my master's wife bare a son to my master when she was old: and unto him hath he given all that he hath
the servant telling of Isaac, the heir of all
the miraculous son of promise born in old age (21:2)
Isaac the sole heir (25:5), the worthy match for Rebekah

schools.summary: The servant recounts the oath against a Canaanite wife: 'and my master made me swear, saying, Thou shalt not take a wife to my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell' — the servant recounting Abraham's oath (24:3): that Isaac was not to marry a Canaanite (the faithful repeating of the charge, the separation of the seed of promise from the accursed, idolatrous line, 9:25, the ground of the errand); the prohibition (24:3) — the heir of promise not to be unequally yoked with the Canaanites (under the curse, idolatrous, the care to preserve the covenant-line, the marrying in the faith); 'in whose land I dwell' (though Abraham dwelt among the Canaanites, he would not have his son marry among them, 24:3, the keeping of the seed separate from the corrupt nations, the godly concern for a wife of the true faith and kindred, the recounted ground of the mission) — Gill.

themes: And my master made me swear... Thou shalt not take a wife to my son of the daughters of the Canaanites, in whose land I dwell
the servant recounting the oath against a Canaanite wife
the heir of promise kept from the accursed, idolatrous line (9:25; 24:3)
the wife to be of the kindred and faith

schools.summary: The servant recounts the charge to seek a wife from the kindred: 'but thou shalt go unto my father's house, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son' — the servant recounting Abraham's charge (24:4): to seek a wife for Isaac from his father's house and kindred (the family of Nahor, 22:20-23, the faithful repeating of the commission, the wife to be of the covenant family, not the Canaanites); the charge to go to Abraham's 'father's house and kindred' (the family of Terah/Nahor in Mesopotamia, from whom Rebekah came, the seeking within the family that retained some knowledge of the true God, the wife of the kindred); 'take a wife unto my son' (the commission to find a wife for the son of promise, 24:4, the marriage through which the covenant-line would continue, the recounted charge that had brought the servant to Nahor's house, the providence guiding the match) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: But thou shalt go unto my father's house, and to my kindred, and take a wife unto my son
the servant recounting the charge to seek a wife from the kindred
the family of Nahor (22:20-23), the wife of the covenant family
the marriage of the son of promise in the faith (24:4)

schools.summary: The servant recounts his question: 'and I said unto my master, Peradventure the woman will not follow me' — the servant recounting his own prudent question (24:5): the possibility that the woman might not be willing to follow him (the faithful repeating of the exchange, the careful foresight of the servant, the question that had drawn out Abraham's answer, 24:6-8); the servant having raised this difficulty (24:5) — the careful, conscientious considering of the difficulties (the prudent servant, the foresight that anticipated the woman's possible refusal); the servant faithfully recounting his question and Abraham's reply (vv.40-41), that the family might know the whole matter and Abraham's confidence in God's guidance (the honest, full account, the providence to be set forth) — Gill.

themes: And I said unto my master, Peradventure the woman will not follow me
the servant recounting his question about the woman's willingness
the careful, prudent foresight of the servant (24:5)
the faithful, full account that the family might know the whole matter

schools.summary: The servant recounts Abraham's faith in the guiding angel: 'and he said unto me, The LORD, before whom I walk, will send his angel with thee, and prosper thy way; and thou shalt take a wife for my son of my kindred, and of my father's house' — the servant recounting Abraham's answer (24:7): the patriarch's confidence that the LORD (before whom he walked, in covenant fellowship, 17:1) would send his angel to guide and prosper the servant's way (the faith in God's guiding providence, the assurance of success); Abraham's 'walking before the LORD' — his covenant fellowship and obedience (17:1, the ground of his confidence, the godly man assured of God's guidance because he walked before him); 'will send his angel with thee, and prosper thy way; and thou shalt take a wife for my son of my kindred' (Abraham's faith that God would send his angel and give success, the assurance that the servant would find a wife from the kindred, 24:7, the recounted confidence in God's providence, the faith that had now been answered, vv.42-48) — Gill.

themes: And he said unto me, The LORD, before whom I walk, will send his angel with thee, and prosper thy way...
the servant recounting Abraham's faith in the guiding angel
Abraham's covenant fellowship - 'the LORD, before whom I walk' (17:1)
the faith in God's guiding providence (24:7), now answered

schools.summary: The servant recounts the release from the oath: 'then shalt thou be clear from this my oath, when thou comest to my kindred; and if they give not thee one, thou shalt be clear from my oath' — the servant recounting the condition Abraham had set (24:8): if the kindred would not give a wife, the servant would be released from his oath (the faithful repeating of the terms, the reasonable limit of the obligation, the servant not bound to the impossible); the servant 'clear' (discharged) if, having come to the kindred, they refused (the conscientious framing of the oath, the servant bound only to make the honest attempt); 'if they give not thee one, thou shalt be clear from my oath' (the release if the kindred refused, the reasonable terms, the recounted condition, now, by God's providence, not needed, the kindred consenting, vv.50-51, the faithful account of the whole charge) — Gill.

themes: Then shalt thou be clear from this my oath, when thou comest to my kindred; and if they give not thee one, thou shalt be clear from my oath
the servant recounting the release from the oath
the reasonable limit of the obligation (24:8)
the recounted condition, now not needed, the kindred consenting (vv.50-51)

schools.summary: The servant recounts his prayer at the well: 'and I came this day unto the well, and said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, if now thou do prosper my way which I go' — the servant recounting his prayer (24:12): coming to the well, he praying to the LORD, the God of his master, to prosper his way (the faithful repeating of his petition, the godly servant's dependence on God, the prayer that had been answered, vv.45-46); the servant's address to 'the LORD God of my master Abraham' (24:12, the true God, whom Abraham served, the piety of the servant, the prayer grounded on God's covenant relation to Abraham); 'if now thou do prosper my way which I go' (the servant's prayer for good success, the seeking of God's guidance, the recounted petition, now seen to have been answered, vv.45-48, the dependence of the servant on providence) — Gill, Clarke, JFB.

themes: And I came this day unto the well, and said, O LORD God of my master Abraham, if now thou do prosper my way which I go
the servant recounting his prayer at the well
the godly servant's dependence on God (24:12)
the recounted petition, now seen to have been answered (vv.45-48)

schools.summary: The servant recounts the sign he proposed: 'behold, I stand by the well of water; and it shall come to pass, that when the virgin cometh forth to draw water, and I say to her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water of thy pitcher to drink' — the servant recounting the sign he had asked (24:13-14): the maiden who, asked for a drink, would offer to water his camels also (the faithful repeating of the providential token, the sign that tested the bride's kindness); the recounting of the maidens coming to draw water (24:13, the setting for the sign, the occasion God would use, the providence about to be set forth); 'Give me... a little water of thy pitcher to drink' (the first part of the proposed sign, 24:14, the asking of a drink, to see if she would offer to water the camels, v.44, the recounted token, the sign that had been exactly fulfilled, vv.45-46) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: Behold, I stand by the well of water... and I say to her, Give me, I pray thee, a little water of thy pitcher to drink
the servant recounting the sign he proposed
the sign that tested the bride's kindness (24:13-14)
the recounted token, now exactly fulfilled (vv.45-46)

schools.summary: The servant recounts the appointed sign: 'and she say to me, Both drink thou, and I will also draw for thy camels: let the same be the woman whom the LORD hath appointed out for my master's son' — the servant recounting the heart of the sign (24:14): the maiden who would offer to water the camels too, let her be the one the LORD had appointed for Isaac (the faithful repeating of the providential token, the sign that revealed the bride); the sign the maiden's offer to water the camels — the test of her kindness, courtesy, and industry (the labor of watering ten camels, the character revealed by the sign, the providential token); 'the woman whom the LORD hath appointed out for my master's son' (the servant looking to the sign as God's appointing of the wife, the faith that God would mark out the appointed bride, the recounted token, now fulfilled in Rebekah, vv.45-46, the providence ordering the match) — Gill.

themes: And she say to me, Both drink thou, and I will also draw for thy camels: let the same be the woman whom the LORD hath appointed out for my master's son
the servant recounting the appointed sign of the camels
the sign that tested the bride's kindness and industry (24:14)
the faith that God would mark out the appointed bride, now fulfilled in Rebekah

schools.summary: The servant recounts the speedy answer: 'and before I had done speaking in mine heart, behold, Rebekah came forth with her pitcher on her shoulder; and she went down unto the well, and drew water: and I said unto her, Let me drink, I pray thee' — the servant recounting how, before he had finished his silent prayer (24:15), Rebekah came (the speedy, immediate answer, Isaiah 65:24, 'in mine heart' marking that his prayer was mental, silent, in the heart, the providence going before the request); the servant's prayer 'in mine heart' (a silent, mental prayer, yet heard and answered, God hears the heart's prayer, 1 Samuel 1:13; Nehemiah 2:4, the prayers of God's people, even unspoken, are heard, the swift answer to the silent petition); 'she went down... and drew water: and I said unto her, Let me drink, I pray thee' (Rebekah coming to draw, and the servant asking the drink, 24:17, the beginning of the sign's fulfillment, the providence bringing the maiden at the very moment) — Ellicott, Gill, Wesley.

themes: And before I had done speaking in mine heart, behold, Rebekah came forth... and I said unto her, Let me drink, I pray thee
the servant recounting the speedy answer to his prayer
the silent, mental prayer ('in mine heart') heard and answered (1 Samuel 1:13)
the immediate answer (Isaiah 65:24), the maiden brought at the very moment

schools.summary: The servant recounts Rebekah's kindness fulfilling the sign: 'and she made haste, and let down her pitcher from her shoulder, and said, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also: so I drank, and she made the camels drink also' — the servant recounting how Rebekah, of her own accord, gave him drink and watered the camels (24:18-20, the exact fulfillment of the sign, 24:14, the maiden's generous, prompt kindness, the providential token given); Rebekah's haste and courtesy — the ready, kind giving of the drink (24:18, the willing, humble response, the character revealed by the sign); 'I will give thy camels drink also: so I drank, and she made the camels drink also' (the very sign the servant had proposed, 24:14, 44, fulfilled to the letter, the maiden watering the camels, the providence confirming Rebekah as the appointed bride, the recounted token) — Gill.

themes: And she made haste... and said, Drink, and I will give thy camels drink also: so I drank, and she made the camels drink also
the servant recounting Rebekah's kindness fulfilling the sign
the exact fulfillment of the sign (24:14, 24:18-20)
the maiden's generous kindness confirming her as the appointed bride

schools.summary: The servant recounts Rebekah's kindred and the gifts: 'and I asked her, and said, Whose daughter art thou? And she said, The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor's son, whom Milcah bare unto him: and I put the earring upon her face, and the bracelets upon her hands' — the servant recounting how he learned Rebekah's family (24:23-24): she the granddaughter of Nahor (Abraham's brother, 22:20-23, the confirmation that she was of the kindred, the providence bringing the right one); Rebekah of the very family Abraham had named (the kindred of Nahor, 24:4, the exact answer to the servant's errand and prayer, the providence confirmed); 'I put the earring upon her face, and the bracelets upon her hands' (the servant recounting the giving of the golden gifts, 24:22, the betrothal-tokens, given when he was sure she was the appointed bride, the honoring of Rebekah, the providence confirmed) — Ellicott, Gill.

themes: And I asked her... Whose daughter art thou? ... The daughter of Bethuel, Nahor's son... and I put the earring upon her face, and the bracelets upon her hands
the servant recounting Rebekah's kindred and the gifts
the maiden of the very kindred of Nahor (22:20-23; 24:4)
honored with the betrothal-gifts (24:22), the providence confirmed

schools.summary: The servant recounts his worship and blessing: 'and I bowed down my head, and worshipped the LORD, and blessed the LORD God of my master Abraham, which had led me in the right way to take my master's brother's daughter unto his son' — the servant recounting his worship (24:26-27): bowing and worshipping the LORD, and blessing him for leading him in the right way (the faithful repeating of his thanksgiving, the godly servant ascribing all to God's guidance); the servant's worship and blessing (24:26-27) — the reverent thanksgiving for the answered prayer (the piety of the servant, the praise for God's guiding providence); 'which had led me in the right way to take my master's brother's daughter unto his son' (God having led the servant in the right way, 24:27, to the daughter of Abraham's brother's son, Bethuel, of Nahor's line, the providence guiding him exactly to the kindred, the recounted acknowledgment of God's leading, the match of God's appointing) — Gill.

themes: And I bowed down my head, and worshipped the LORD... which had led me in the right way to take my master's brother's daughter unto his son
the servant recounting his worship and blessing of the LORD
the reverent thanksgiving for the answered prayer (24:26-27)
God's leading in the right way to the kindred, the match of God's appointing

schools.summary: The servant presses for an answer: 'and now if ye will deal kindly and truly with my master, tell me: and if not, tell me; that I may turn to the right hand, or to the left' — the servant, having told his errand (vv.34-48), pressing the family for a plain answer: would they deal kindly (with favor) and truly (faithfully, honestly) with Abraham in giving Rebekah? (the frank request for a clear decision, the faithful servant seeking the resolution of his commission); the appeal for 'kindness and truth' (favor and faithfulness) — the servant asking the family to deal honorably and faithfully with Abraham (the plain, honest request, the seeking of a fair answer); 'that I may turn to the right hand, or to the left' (the servant seeking a clear answer, that he might know how to proceed, whether to stay and take Rebekah, or to turn elsewhere, the diligent, decisive seeking of the matter's resolution, the faithful steward pressing for a decision, the way opened to the family's consent, vv.50-51) — Gill, Clarke.

themes: And now if ye will deal kindly and truly with my master, tell me: and if not, tell me; that I may turn to the right hand, or to the left
the servant pressing the family for an answer
the frank request for a plain, honest decision
the way opened to the family's consent (vv.50-51)

schools.summary: The family confesses the matter is of the LORD: 'then Laban and Bethuel answered and said, The thing proceedeth from the LORD: we cannot speak unto thee bad or good' — the family (Laban the brother, Bethuel the father) answering the servant (Laban named first, perhaps as the more forward, or as conducting the matter, as later, chs. 29-31, the family's response to the servant's account); 'the thing proceedeth from the LORD' (the family confessing that the matter was of the LORD, his providence so evidently directing the whole, the prayer, the sign, the leading, the acknowledgment of God's hand in the marriage, even Laban and Bethuel, whose house was not free of idolatry, 31:19, 30, owning the providence — so plain was God's guiding, the confession that God had ordered the match); 'we cannot speak unto thee bad or good' (the family could neither speak against the match, bad, nor add anything of their own, good — that is, they could not gainsay or alter what God had so evidently appointed, the submission to the providence, the acknowledgment that the matter was settled by God, not to be resisted) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

debates.question: What does the family's 'the thing proceedeth from the LORD' confess?

sides.position: The providence of God owned - his hand in the match so plain that even this household confessed it; 'we cannot speak bad or good' = they could neither gainsay nor alter what God had appointed, the submission to providence

themes: Then Laban and Bethuel answered and said, The thing proceedeth from the LORD: we cannot speak unto thee bad or good
the family's confession that the matter is of the LORD (the crux of the providence owned)
God's hand in the match so plain that even this household owned it
'we cannot speak bad or good' - they could neither gainsay nor alter what God had appointed

schools.summary: The family gives Rebekah: 'behold, Rebekah is before thee, take her, and go, and let her be thy master's son's wife, as the LORD hath spoken' — the family freely giving Rebekah to be Isaac's wife (the consent to the match, the maiden placed at the servant's disposal, subject to her own consent, vv.57-58, the granting of the bride); the family agreeing she should be 'thy master's son's wife' (Isaac's wife, the consent to the marriage, the bride given to the heir of promise, the match concluded, pending Rebekah's consent); 'as the LORD hath spoken' (the family owning that the match was as the LORD had directed, v.50, the marriage according to God's evident appointing, the consent given in submission to the providence, the acknowledgment that God had spoken, by the providence, in ordering the match) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: Behold, Rebekah is before thee, take her, and go, and let her be thy master's son's wife, as the LORD hath spoken
the family giving Rebekah as the LORD had spoken
the free consent to the marriage, the bride given to the heir of promise
the match owned as God's appointing (v.50)

schools.summary: The servant worships upon the consent: 'and it came to pass, that, when Abraham's servant heard their words, he worshipped the LORD, bowing himself to the earth' — upon hearing the family's consent (vv.50-51), the servant again worshipping the LORD, bowing to the earth (the prompt, reverent thanksgiving for the success of his errand, the godly servant turning at once to worship, the second worship, cf. 24:26, marking his piety); the servant 'worshipped the LORD' (in thanksgiving for the answered prayer and the consent, the reverent praise, the example of the godly, returning thanks at each step of God's providence, the piety of the steward); 'bowing himself to the earth' (the deep, humble gesture of worship and gratitude, the servant's lowly adoration, the thanksgiving for God's mercy and faithfulness, the worship that fittingly answered the family's confession, v.50) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And it came to pass, that, when Abraham's servant heard their words, he worshipped the LORD, bowing himself to the earth
the servant worshipping the LORD upon the family's consent
the prompt, reverent thanksgiving for the success of his errand (24:26)
the example of the godly, returning thanks at each step of God's providence

schools.summary: The servant gives betrothal-gifts: 'and the servant brought forth jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment, and gave them to Rebekah: he gave also to her brother and to her mother precious things' — the servant giving Rebekah rich gifts (silver and gold ornaments and costly garments, the betrothal-presents, the dowry-gifts, the tokens of the espousal, the honoring of the bride, the wealth of Abraham bestowed on Rebekah); the rich gifts (jewels and raiment) — the customary betrothal-presents (the espousal-gifts marking Rebekah as the affianced bride, the tokens of the honorable match, the adorning of the bride); 'he gave also to her brother and to her mother precious things' (the servant giving gifts to Laban and to Rebekah's mother, the customary presents to the family of the bride, the honoring of the household, the espousal concluded with gifts) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And the servant brought forth jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment, and gave them to Rebekah: he gave also to her brother and to her mother precious things
the servant giving betrothal-gifts to Rebekah and her family
the rich betrothal-presents adorning the bride and honoring the family
the espousal concluded with gifts

schools.summary: The servant's eagerness to return: 'and they did eat and drink, he and the men that were with him, and tarried all night; and they rose up in the morning, and he said, Send me away unto my master' — the espousal concluded, the servant and his men eating, drinking, and lodging the night (the hospitality of the household, the rest after the day's business, the staying the night before the morning's request); in the morning the servant saying, 'Send me away unto my master' (his eagerness to return at once with the bride, the faithful steward's diligence, not lingering, but hastening to bring his errand to its end, the zeal to report to his master, the dispatch of the faithful servant); 'Send me away unto my master' (the servant pressing to depart, that he might bring Rebekah to Isaac without delay, the diligence in God's work, the faithful servant's care to finish his task, the request that met the family's wish to delay, v.55) — Gill, Henry, Calvin.

themes: And they did eat and drink... and tarried all night; and they rose up in the morning, and he said, Send me away unto my master
the servant's eagerness to return to his master
the faithful steward's diligence, not lingering but hastening
the zeal to finish his errand and report to his master

schools.summary: The family wishes to delay: 'and her brother and her mother said, Let the damsel abide with us a few days, at the least ten; after that she shall go' — the family (Laban and the mother) wishing Rebekah to stay a while (some days, or ten) before leaving (the natural desire to keep her a little longer before the parting, the affection of the family, the request to delay); the request for 'a few days, at the least ten' (or, by some renderings, a year or ten months, the family's wish for some time with Rebekah before her departure, the natural reluctance to part suddenly, the affectionate request); 'after that she shall go' (the family not refusing, but only seeking to delay, they consenting she should go, but wishing a little time first, the affectionate desire met by the servant's haste, v.56, and Rebekah's own consent, v.58) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Clarke, Ellicott.

themes: And her brother and her mother said, Let the damsel abide with us a few days, at the least ten; after that she shall go
the family's wish to delay Rebekah's departure
the natural affection and reluctance to part suddenly
the consent, only seeking a little time before the parting

schools.summary: The servant presses for departure: 'and he said unto them, Hinder me not, seeing the LORD hath prospered my way; send me away that I may go to my master' — the servant urging the family not to hinder him, since the LORD had prospered his way (the faithful steward pressing for a prompt departure, the diligence in finishing his errand, the ground of his haste — that God had given success, and he would not delay); the servant's reason — 'the LORD hath prospered my way' (the success of his errand the ground for haste, the diligence of the servant who, seeing God's hand, would carry the matter to its end without delay, the faith that pressed forward); 'send me away that I may go to my master' (the servant's eagerness to return to Abraham with the bride, the faithful care to finish his task, the diligence in God's work, the request that led to the calling of Rebekah, v.57) — Gill.

themes: And he said unto them, Hinder me not, seeing the LORD hath prospered my way; send me away that I may go to my master
the servant pressing for a prompt departure
the success of his errand the ground for haste
the faithful diligence that pressed forward in God's work

schools.summary: The family refers the matter to Rebekah: 'and they said, We will call the damsel, and enquire at her mouth' — the family proposing to call Rebekah and ask her own mind (whether she would go at once, the referring of the decision to the maiden herself, the respect for her consent, the matter put to Rebekah's choice); the family would 'call the damsel' (they would not compel her, but ask her, the respect for the bride's own consent in the marriage, the freedom of Rebekah to choose, the godly regard for her will); 'enquire at her mouth' (they would ask Rebekah's own decision, the matter referred to her free consent, the maiden's own choice respected, as marriage should be by consent, the way to Rebekah's willing 'I will go,' v.58) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And they said, We will call the damsel, and enquire at her mouth
the family referring the matter to Rebekah's own choice
the respect for the bride's own consent in the marriage
the way to Rebekah's willing answer (v.58)

schools.summary: Rebekah's willing consent: 'and they called Rebekah, and said unto her, Wilt thou go with this man? And she said, I will go' — the family asking Rebekah her own choice: would she go (at once) with the servant to become Isaac's wife? (the referring of the decision to her free consent, v.57, the maiden's own will sought); Rebekah answering, 'I will go' (her free, prompt, decided consent, 'it fully appears to be her own free choice... and I consent,' her readiness to go at once, though it meant leaving her home and kindred for a land she had not seen, to marry a man she had not met, the willing resolve, the faith and decision of the maiden, the consent that at once determined the matter); Rebekah's willing 'I will go' — her ready obedience to the call, leaving all to go to the bridegroom (some finding here a figure of the soul's, the church's, consent to follow the call of Christ, leaving the world to go to the unseen Bridegroom, cf. Psalm 45:10, 'forget also thine own people, and thy father's house,' the faith and willingness of the bride, the providence carrying the match forward by her free consent) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

debates.question: What is the significance of Rebekah's 'I will go'?

sides.position: The free, prompt, decided consent of the bride - her readiness to leave home and kindred at once for the unseen bridegroom, her faith and willing resolve
A figure of the soul's (the church's) consent to follow the call of Christ, leaving the world for the unseen Bridegroom (Psalm 45:10)

themes: And they called Rebekah, and said unto her, Wilt thou go with this man? And she said, I will go
Rebekah's willing consent (the crux of 'I will go')
the free, prompt, decided consent of the bride, ready to leave home for the unseen bridegroom
some finding a figure of the soul's consent to follow Christ (Psalm 45:10)

schools.summary: The sending away of Rebekah with her nurse: 'and they sent away Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham's servant, and his men' — the family sending Rebekah away (with their blessing, v.60) to go to Isaac, along with her nurse and the servant's company (the departure of the bride, the nurse, Deborah, named later, 35:8, accompanying her, the bride sent forth to the marriage); Rebekah's nurse (Deborah, 35:8) going with her — a token of the family's care, and of Rebekah's honored place (the faithful nurse accompanying the bride, the company sent with her); 'Abraham's servant, and his men' (the servant and his men, their errand accomplished, setting out to bring Rebekah home, the company departing, the bride conducted to Isaac, the providence carrying the match to its end) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin.

themes: And they sent away Rebekah their sister, and her nurse, and Abraham's servant, and his men
the sending away of Rebekah with her nurse
the bride sent forth to the marriage, her nurse (Deborah, 35:8) with her
the providence carrying the match to its end
