schools.summary: The blessing of Rebekah: 'and they blessed Rebekah, and said unto her, Thou art our sister, be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those which hate them' — the family blessing Rebekah at her departure with a wish (and, providentially, a prophecy) of a vast posterity: that she might be the mother of thousands of millions (innumerable descendants, the customary blessing of the bride, the wish for fruitfulness); the blessing of a numberless seed — 'thousands of millions' (the wish, echoing God's promise to Abraham, 22:17, for an innumerable posterity, the blessing fulfilled in Rebekah's seed, Israel, and the spiritual seed, the providence in the family's words); 'let thy seed possess the gate of those which hate them' (the wish, echoing 22:17, the very promise to Abraham, that Rebekah's seed would possess the gate of their enemies, the victory and dominion of the seed, the family's blessing remarkably echoing God's covenant-promise, Israel's victory, and supremely Christ's over his enemies, the providence speaking through the blessing) — Ellicott, Gill, JFB, Clarke.

themes: And they blessed Rebekah... be thou the mother of thousands of millions, and let thy seed possess the gate of those which hate them
the blessing of Rebekah with a numberless seed
the wish for an innumerable, victorious posterity
the blessing remarkably echoing God's promise to Abraham (22:17)

schools.summary: Rebekah sets out with the servant: 'and Rebekah arose, and her damsels, and they rode upon the camels, and followed the man: and the servant took Rebekah, and went his way' — Rebekah and her maids rising, mounting the camels, and following the servant (the bride setting out for the land of Canaan, the servant taking Rebekah and departing, the journey to Isaac begun); Rebekah and her damsels 'followed the man' (the bride following the servant to her bridegroom, the willing departure, v.58, the journey to the unseen husband, the obedience of the bride); 'the servant took Rebekah, and went his way' (the servant, his errand accomplished, taking the bride and setting out for Canaan, the homeward journey, the providence carrying Rebekah to Isaac, the bride conducted to the marriage) — Gill, Wesley.

themes: And Rebekah arose, and her damsels, and they rode upon the camels, and followed the man: and the servant took Rebekah, and went his way
Rebekah setting out with the servant
the bride following to her bridegroom (v.58)
the providence carrying Rebekah to Isaac, the journey begun

schools.summary: Isaac comes from the well Lahai-roi: 'and Isaac came from the way of the well Lahairoi; for he dwelt in the south country' — Isaac coming from (the region of) the well Lahai-roi ('the well of the Living One who seeth me,' 16:14, where the Angel had met Hagar, Isaac dwelling in the south country, the Negev, the setting for his meeting with Rebekah, the place of the well associated with God's seeing and providence); the well Lahai-roi (16:14) — 'the well of him that liveth and seeth me' (the place where God had revealed himself to Hagar, the well near which Isaac dwelt, the place of God's providence, fitting for the meeting); 'he dwelt in the south country' (Isaac dwelling in the Negev, the south of Canaan, the place to which Rebekah was being brought, the setting for the meeting, vv.63-65, the providence bringing the bride to the bridegroom's land) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill, Henry, Ellicott, Clarke.

themes: And Isaac came from the way of the well Lahairoi; for he dwelt in the south country
Isaac coming from the well Lahai-roi
the well of him that liveth and seeth me (16:14), the place of God's providence
the setting for the meeting with Rebekah in the south country

schools.summary: Isaac goes out to meditate: 'and Isaac went out to meditate in the field at the eventide: and he lifted up his eyes, and saw, and, behold, the camels were coming' — the Hebrew lasuach (rendered 'to meditate') meaning to muse, to bend oneself in thought — variously rendered 'to meditate' or 'to pray': Isaac going out into the field at evening to meditate (to muse in holy contemplation) or to pray (the place, the field; the time, the eventide; both proper for devotion, the godly habit of meditation/prayer, some taking it of meditation, holy musing on God and his works, others of prayer, the field and evening fit for private devotion, perhaps praying about the very matter of his marriage, the subject of his meditation not told, it is useless to inquire, but his devotion plain); the place and time fit for devotion — the field (solitude) and the eventide (evening, a time of prayer, Isaac's habit of retiring for meditation or prayer, the godly soul communing with God, some, his prayers about a wife now quickly answered, the camels appearing); 'he lifted up his eyes, and saw, and, behold, the camels were coming' (as Isaac meditated, he seeing the camels approaching, Rebekah coming, the providence bringing the bride at the very time of his devotion, the meeting at hand) — Clarke, Wesley, Calvin, Gill.

debates.question: What does 'Isaac went out to meditate' (lasuach) mean?

sides.position: To meditate - holy musing, contemplation on God and his works (the field and eventide fit for devotion)
To pray - private devotion (the field and evening fit for prayer, perhaps about his marriage, quickly answered)

themes: And Isaac went out to meditate in the field at the eventide: and he lifted up his eyes, and saw, and, behold, the camels were coming
Isaac going out to meditate in the field (the crux of 'meditate')
lasuach - to meditate (holy musing) or to pray (private devotion)
the godly soul communing with God at evening, the camels appearing

schools.summary: Rebekah alights at the sight of Isaac: 'and Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she lighted off the camel' — Rebekah, seeing Isaac (whom she learned was her bridegroom, v.65), alighting from the camel (the gesture of respect and modesty toward her future husband, the bride's reverence, the customary token of honor, dismounting before a superior); Rebekah seeing Isaac (the man approaching, v.65) — the first sight of her bridegroom (the meeting of bride and groom, the providence bringing them together); 'she lighted off the camel' (Rebekah dismounting, a token of respect and modesty toward Isaac, the bride's reverence and humility, the customary courtesy, the meeting of the bride and the bridegroom) — Gill, Calvin, Wesley.

themes: And Rebekah lifted up her eyes, and when she saw Isaac, she lighted off the camel
Rebekah alighting at the sight of Isaac
the bride's token of respect and modesty toward her bridegroom
the meeting of bride and groom

schools.summary: Rebekah veils herself before Isaac: 'for she had said unto the servant, What man is this that walketh in the field to meet us? And the servant had said, It is my master: therefore she took a vail, and covered herself' — Rebekah asking who the man approaching was, and the servant telling her it was Isaac (his master, the bridegroom, the recognition of Isaac, the prompting of her modest veiling); the servant calling Isaac 'my master' (the heir, now the servant's young master, identifying the bridegroom, the bride's learning who Isaac was, the meeting at hand); 'she took a vail, and covered herself' (Rebekah veiling herself before her bridegroom, the token of modesty and of the bridal custom, the bride veiled before the groom until the marriage, cf. 29:23-25, the modesty and reverence of the bride, the customary veiling) — Ellicott, Gill, Clarke.

themes: For she had said unto the servant, What man is this that walketh in the field to meet us? And the servant had said, It is my master: therefore she took a vail, and covered herself
Rebekah veiling herself before Isaac
the token of modesty and of the bridal custom (cf. 29:23)
the reverence and modesty of the bride

schools.summary: The servant reports all to Isaac: 'and the servant told Isaac all things that he had done' — the servant giving Isaac a full account of his errand (the oath, the journey, the prayer, the sign, the providence, the betrothal, the faithful steward reporting to his, young, master, the recounting of God's guidance in the whole matter, the account that would assure Isaac of the providence in his marriage); the servant telling Isaac 'all things' — the whole account of the providence (vv.12-49, the faithful report, Isaac learning how God had guided the servant to Rebekah, the providence made known); 'all things that he had done' (the servant's full narration of the errand and the evident hand of God, Isaac assured that his bride was of God's appointing, the providence acknowledged, the way to the marriage, v.67) — Gill, JFB.

themes: And the servant told Isaac all things that he had done
the servant reporting all to Isaac
the faithful steward's full account of God's guidance in the errand
Isaac assured that his bride was of God's appointing

schools.summary: The marriage of Isaac and Rebekah, and his comfort: 'and Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah's tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife; and he loved her: and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death' — Isaac bringing Rebekah into the tent that had been Sarah's (the matriarch's place now filled by the new wife, he taking Rebekah, and she becoming his wife, the marriage consummated/completed, Rebekah installed as the matriarch in Sarah's tent, the succession of the covenant-mother); Isaac 'loved her' (true conjugal love, as a husband ought to love his wife, Ephesians 5:25, the affection that grew in the marriage, the love of Isaac for Rebekah, the godly marriage of love); 'Isaac was comforted after his mother's death' (Isaac, who had mourned Sarah, she having died three years before, when Isaac was thirty-seven, he now forty, 25:20, finding comfort in Rebekah, the new wife a comfort for the lost mother, the tender close of the history, some finding in the whole a type — Isaac, the son, raised as it were from the dead, ch. 22, receiving a bride brought by the servant, a figure of Christ and the church, the bride gathered for the Son by the Spirit's work, the marriage that carried the covenant-line forward) — Ellicott, Gill, Calvin, Henry.

debates.question: What does the close - the marriage and Isaac's comfort - display?

sides.position: The marriage completed (Rebekah installed in Sarah's tent, the covenant-mother's place filled), Isaac's love, and his comfort after his mother's death (the new wife a comfort for the lost mother)
A type of Christ and the church - the son (raised as from the dead, ch. 22) receiving a bride gathered for him by the servant

themes: And Isaac brought her into his mother Sarah's tent, and took Rebekah, and she became his wife; and he loved her: and Isaac was comforted after his mother's death
the marriage of Isaac and Rebekah and his comfort (the crux of the consummation and comfort)
Rebekah installed in Sarah's tent, the covenant-mother's place filled; Isaac's love (Ephesians 5:25)
Isaac comforted after Sarah's death; some finding a type of Christ and the church
